coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of red carbon quantum dots with solvatochromic properties as a selective luminescence probe of MnO4 −
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

coredata.dc:creator: Chu, Wen-Xin
Tang, Xiao-Min
Lu, Fei-Yang
An, Bao-Li
Zhang, Ji-Ming
Wang, Xiao-Hong
Bai, Yue-Ling
Xu, Jiaqiang
coredata.dc:description: Red efficient luminescent carbon quantum dots are still difficult to be synthesized. In this work, red carbon quantum dots (RCQDs) isopropanol solution with quantum yield of 43.9 % have been synthesized using isopropanol as carbon source and solvent, N1-phenylbenzene-1,2,4-triamine as carbon and nitrogen source. The solvothermal synthesis was catalyzed surprisingly by SnO2 nanoparticles, the optimal synthesis temperature was lowered to 130 °C, and the optimal synthesis time was reduced to 1 h. Moreover, the RCQDs nanoparticles showed excellent solvatochromic properties. The maximum emission wavelength for RCQDs in N, N-dimethylformamide dilute solution blue shifted to 504 nm, and the maximum emission wavelengths for RCQDs in acetone, isopropanol, dimethyl sulfoxide and water solutions were in the range of 592 nm ∼ 624 nm. The reason was investigated by the in-situ infrared absorption spectra. MnO4 − ions could be selectively detected using RCQDs in PBS buffer solution (pH = 5.0), the linear range of MnO4 − concentration was 0.1 μM ∼ 2.0 μM. This work provides a new synthesis method of efficient red carbon quantum dots, and it supplies a friendly candidate for detection of MnO4 − ions in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Red luminescence
Solvatochromic
Luminescence probe
MnO4 -

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the synthesis, the solvatochromic properties of RCQDs, the luminescence change as RCQDs mixed with MnO4 − ions.

(a) The TEM image of RCQDs. (b) The HRTEM image of RCQDs, the top inset was the FFT pattern of RCQDs. (c) The particle size distribution of RCQDs. (d) The XRD patterns of RCQDs. (e) The XRD patterns of the synchronously synthesized SnO2 NPs. (f) The Raman spectrum of RCQDs. (g) Full-survey XPS spectrum of RCQDs (g), high-resolution spectra for C 1 s (h), N 1 s (i) and O 1 s (j).
The FT-IR spectrum of RCQDs, the in-situ FT-IR spectra of RCQD-IPA, RCQD-DMF, RCQD-acetone, RCQD-DMSO and RCQD-H2O.
UV–vis spectra for RCQDs in aqueous solution (RCQD-H2O), DMSO solution (RCQD-DMSO), isopropanol solution (RCQD-IPA), acetone solution (RCQD-acetone) and DMF solution (RCQD-DMF).
The surface defect state energies for RCQDs in DMF (a), acetone (b), isopropanol (c), DMSO (d), and water (e) solutions were calculated from their UV–vis spectra.
The excitation spectra (a) and the emission spectra (b) for 0.040 mg/ml RCQDs in DMF, acetone, isopropanol, DMSO and water dilute solutions.
The lifetime decay curves for RCQDs in DMF (a), acetone (b), isopropanol (c), DMSO (d) and H2O (e) dilute solutions.

(a) The emission spectra for 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs in different pH buffer solutions. (b) The curve for the maximum emission intensities for 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs versus the pH values. (c) The emission spectra for 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs isopropanol solution mixed with different content (V%) of PBS buffer solution (pH = 5.0). (d) The curve for the I/I 0 versus the content of PBS buffer solution. (e) The emission spectra for 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs isopropanol solution mixed with 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l MnO4 − in 50 V% PBS buffer solution. (f) The curve for the I/I 0 of 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs isopropanol solution mixed with 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l MnO4 − versus the time.

(a) The emission spectra for 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs in the mixed PBS buffer solution with different concentrations of MnO4 −. (b) The curve for the I/I0 of 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs solution versus the mixed concentration of MnO4 −. (c) The curve for the I/I0 of 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs solution versus the mixed concentration of MnO4 − in the range of 1.0 × 10−7 mol/l ∼ 2.0 × 10−6 mol/l.

The I/I0 values of 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs solutions at 612 nm after addition of 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l MnO4 −, some cations (a) or some anions (b) respectively. The I/I0 values of 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs solutions at 612 nm under coexistence of MnO4 − together with some cations (c) or some anions (d) respectively. All the solutions in PBS buffer solution (pH = 5.0).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra for 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs (black line), 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l MnO4 − (blue line), and 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs mixed with 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l MnO4 − (violet line). (b) The lifetime decay curves for 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs (black line), 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs mixed with 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l MnO4 − (blue line). All the solutions were in PBS buffer solution (pH = 5.0).
The quantum yield data and the FWHM values for RCQDs in different solvent solutions.
The lifetime data for RCQDs in different solvent solutions.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of red carbon quantum dots with solvatochromic properties as a selective luminescence probe of MnO4 −

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444 China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Periodicals Agency, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444 China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Periodicals Agency, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red carbon quantum dots with great solvatochromic properties as a selective luminescence probe of MnO4 − ions.

Red efficient luminescent carbon quantum dots are still difficult to be synthesized. In this work, red carbon quantum dots (RCQDs) isopropanol solution with quantum yield of 43.9 % have been synthesized using isopropanol as carbon source and solvent, N1-phenylbenzene-1,2,4-triamine as carbon and nitrogen source. The solvothermal synthesis was catalyzed surprisingly by SnO2 nanoparticles, the optimal synthesis temperature was lowered to 130 °C, and the optimal synthesis time was reduced to 1 h. Moreover, the RCQDs nanoparticles showed excellent solvatochromic properties. The maximum emission wavelength for RCQDs in N, N-dimethylformamide dilute solution blue shifted to 504 nm, and the maximum emission wavelengths for RCQDs in acetone, isopropanol, dimethyl sulfoxide and water solutions were in the range of 592 nm ∼ 624 nm. The reason was investigated by the in-situ infrared absorption spectra. MnO4 − ions could be selectively detected using RCQDs in PBS buffer solution (pH = 5.0), the linear range of MnO4 − concentration was 0.1 μM ∼ 2.0 μM. This work provides a new synthesis method of efficient red carbon quantum dots, and it supplies a friendly candidate for detection of MnO4 − ions in water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) won the 2023 Nobel prize in chemistry [1]. Semiconductor quantum dots such as CdSe/CdTe pollute the earth's environment and impact human health [2,3]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as metal-free quantum dots, have attracted much attention due to their excellent biocompatibility, tunable photo-luminescence, good photo-stability [4–6]. Moreover, they have great potential applications in bio-imaging, fluorescence probe, anti-counterfeiting, luminescent solar concentrators etc. [7–9]. CQDs have been synthesized by various methods, pyrolysis, hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis, microwave synthesis and solid phase method [10–12]. However, mono-dispersed efficient luminescent CQDs have been synthesized by hydrothermal/solvothermal method. Excitingly, multi-color visible and near-infrared CQDs have been synthesized by hydrothermal method [13,14]. The quantum yield for red CQDs with large π-conjugated structure have reached 86 % [15], however, the synthesis time required 12 h at 200 °C. Presently, it is of great significance to investigate a new method to synthesize highly efficient red CQDs lowering synthesis cost by reducing the synthesis time and the synthesis temperature.

The synthesis method for multicolor CQDs have been reviewed [16]. However, some functional groups on the surfaces of CQDs are apt to react with solvent molecules, and the solvent molecule affect the surface defect state energies of CQDs. Sometimes, the polarity of solvent molecules might affect the surface defect states of CQDs. A nitrogen functionalized carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) showed solvatochromic properties (450 nm to 502 nm), the reason was attributed to the hydrogen bonding between the N-CDs and the protic solvents [17]. A red graphene quantum dots showed good solvatochromic properties (532 nm to 624 nm) [18]. The maximum absorption wavelengths in visible region for the graphene quantum dots in some solvents showed visible red-shift with increased polarities of the solvents, and their emission spectra were independent on the excitation wavelengths. Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) as an important strong oxidizing agent, is widely used in disinfectant, water treatment. Excessive intake of MnO4 − can seriously impact human health, causing abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, even death by suffocation [19]. The tolerable concentration limit of MnO4 − was proposed to be 7 mg/l (44.3 μmol/l) according to the clinical data [20]. The concentration limit of Mn element in groundwater was proposed to be 0.4 mg/l (7.2 μmol/l) by the World Health Organization [21]. Currently, the analysis methods for MnO4 − ions are flame atomic absorption spectrometry, electrochemiluminescence, spectrophotometry [22–24], however, these methods require expensive instruments, complex processes. Fluorescence or phosphorescence methods using CQDs, semiconductor QDs, polymer nanoparticles, MOF as luminescence materials, and the main reported methods have been summarized in Table S4. The CQDs generally have variety of functional groups on their surfaces, these functional groups can be used to detect some anions/cations, or molecules by luminescence enhancement or luminescence quench process [25]. The concentration of MnO4 − has been detected using red CQDs as luminescence probe, and the limit of detection was 0.25 μmol/l, and the linear range was 0 ∼ 7 μM [26]. The detection limit for MnO4 − was 0.17 μM with violet CQDs, and the linear range was 0.51 ∼ 2 μM by dynamic quenching [27]. The detection limit for MnO4 − was 0.05 μM with blue-purple CQDs co-doped N/S/P by internal filter effect (IFE) mechanism, and the linear range was 0.05 ∼ 20 μM [28]. The detection limit for MnO4 − was 0.72 μM with blue CQDs using both IFE and static quenching mechanism, and the linear range was 0 ∼ 20 μM [29].

In our published work, SnO2 nanoparticles (NPs) showed excellent catalytic effect on the pyrolysis of the isopropanol which was used as the carbon source and solvent, and the pyrolysis mechanism of isopropanol was proposed [30]. The catalysis results of SnO2 NPs with some precursors to the emission color and the quantum yields of some CQDs were further investigated and discussed detailed [31]. In this work, N1-phenylbenzene-1,2,4-triamine with larger conjugated structure was selected as one of the precursors, RCQDs have been synthesized by solvothermal method at 130 °C for 1 h using SnO2 NPs as a catalyst. Compared with those synthesis conditions using conventional solvothermal methods, both the solvothermal temperature and the synthesis time were greatly reduced. Moreover, the maximum quantum yield for the RCQDs isopropanol dilute solution reached 43.9 %, and the maximum emission wavelength was 608 nm. The RCQDs showed excellent solvatochromic properties, and the maximum emission wavelengths of RCQDs in dilute N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and water were 504 nm, 592 nm, 610 nm and 624 nm respectively, exhibiting wide solvatochromic range of 120 nm. The maximum quantum yields for RCQDs in DMF, acetone, DMSO and water dilute solution were 55.2 %, 36.9 %, 32.1 %, 8.1 % respectively. The luminescence of RCQDs was quenched selectively by MnO4 −. The relative luminescence intensity I/I0 had a good linear relationship with the concentration of MnO4 −, and the linear range was 0.1 ∼ 2.0 μM. The response time was lower than 3 min, and its detection limit was lower 1 order compared with those using MOF as the luminescent materials (Table S4).

N1-phenylbenzene-1,2,4-triamine (98 %), SnCl4·5H2O (98 %) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd, isopropanol (IPA, 99.7 %) was purchased from Titan Scientific Co., Ltd, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), hexane, toluene, dichloromethane (DCM), tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethyl acetate (EA) and acetonitrile (ACN) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the inorganic salts, KNO3, NaF, Na2CO3, NiCl2·6H2O, NaHCO3, K2C2O4, KBr, FeCl3·6H2O, ZnCl2, NaNO2, BaCl2·2H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, Na2HPO4, NaCl, Na2S2O8, KMnO4, K2Cr2O7 and CdSO4 were analytical purity. Deionized water was obtained from the laboratory Millipore-Q system. All chemicals were used directly without any purification. PBS buffer solution (pH = 5.0) was obtained by mixing NaH2PO4 solution and NaOH solution, and the pH value was controlled using a pH meter.

19.93 mg (0.1 mmol) N1-phenylbenzene-1,2,4-triamine and 26.29 mg (0.075 mmol) SnCl4·5H2O were dissolved in 30 ml IPA, and the mixed solution was stirred at 60 °C for 1 h until a colloidal solution of SnO2 NPs was obtained, and the pH of the mixed solution was 5. Then, the mixed solution was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, and the mixed solution was kept at 130 °C for 1 h. The solution was cooled to room temperature. The SnO2 NPs were removed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min. Then, the soluble small molecules were wiped out by dialysis for 48 h using 3500 Da dialysis bags. 2 mg/ml RCQDs isopropanol solution were obtained (30 ml), then, the RCQDs solution was stored in refrigerator at 4 °C for further testing. The synthesis schematic illustration is shown in Fig. 1 .

The morphology of the RCQDs were characterized by a JEM F200 transmission electron microscope (TEM) with accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The infrared absorption spectra were measured by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, AVATAR 370) using the KBr method. UV–visible absorption spectra were measured by TU-1950 UV–visible spectrophotometer. The excitation / emission spectra were tested by Edinburgh FLS 1000 transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer. The luminescence lifetimes were determined by Edinburgh FLS 1000 using EPL-visible 450 nm laser as the excitation source. The luminescence quantum yield data were obtained by FLS 1000 with a red PMT sphere. The XRD spectra were carried out with a powder diffractometer (DX2700) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54184 Å). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on Thermo ESCALAB 250 with an Al anode as Kα source. Raman spectrum was obtained on laser Raman spectrometer (Renishaw in Via plus) using 532 nm laser as the excitation source.

The detection of MnO4 − was carried out in 0.020 mol/l PBS buffer solution (pH = 5.0) at room temperature. In a typical experiment, 25.00 ml 0.040 mg/ml RCQDs isopropanol solution was diluted to a total volume of 50.00 ml by 0.020 mol/l PBS buffer solution. Then, 1.0 × 10−7 mol/l ∼ 3.0 × 10−5 mol/l MnO4 − was added into 3.0 ml the diluted RCQDs solution. After 3 min, the emission spectra of the samples were recorded under excitation at 574 nm, and all the emission data were obtained under the same parameters of the FLS 1000.

The selectivity experiments of RCQDs were done by adding different anions in PBS buffer solution (0.020 mol/l, pH = 5.0) at room temperature. In a typical experiment, 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l NO3 −, F−, CO3 2−, Cl−, HCO3 −, C2O4 2−, Br−, NO2 −, HPO4 2−, S2O8 2−, MnO4 −, Cr2O7 2− and SO4 2− in aqueous solutions were mixed with 3.00 ml diluted RCQDs solution. All the emission spectra of the samples were recorded after they were placed in air for 3 min. The emission spectra for the samples containing 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l K+, Na+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cu2+ and Cd2+ were obtained using the same method as described for the anions.

The interference tests were carried out by detecting the relative PL intensities of the coexistence samples of MnO4 − and the above mentioned each anion and each cation. The concentration of MnO4 − was 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l, and the concentration of each interference ion was also 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l. The test conditions were as same as those for the detection of each ion.

First, the maximum excitation wavelength for the samples was set, then the emission spectra for the reference blank (solvent solution) and the sample solution were recorded respectively by setting the emission range. The absolute quantum yields were calculated based on the following Eq. (2-1) by FLS 1000.
(2-1)
Q Y =
E s
-
E r
S r
-
S s
Here, QY is absolute quantum yields, E s and E r are the integral emission photon counts for the sample solution and the reference solvent solution respectively in the emission range. S r and Ss are the integral photon counts for the reference solvent solution and the sample solution respectively in the excitation range (around in 5 nm).

The TEM image of RCQDs was given in Fig. 2 a, and the shape was close to spherical. The nano particles had uniform particle size distribution, and aggregation phenomenon was not found. The high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) of RCQDs showed good crystallinity with clear lattice stripes (Fig. 2b). The lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, which was attributed to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphitic carbon [32]. The particle distribution of RCQDs was shown in Fig. 2c, the main size distribution was 2.75 ∼ 3.75 nm with the average particle size of 3.38 nm. The inset in Fig. 2b was the fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern of the HRTEM image, and it was further evidence of the highly crystallinity of RCQDs. The XRD pattern of RCQDs showed a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 26.6°, which was attributed to the (002) diffraction patterns of the graphitic carbon (Fig. 2d).

The synchronously synthesized SnO2 NPs were mainly cassiterite SnO2 based on their XRD pattern (Fig. 2e). The mean size of the cassiterite SnO2 NPs was calculated to be about 3.2 nm by the Scherrer’s equation (3-1) as follows:
(3-1)
D =
0.89 λ
β cos θ
Here, λ is the wavelength of the X-ray (λ = 0.154 nm), β is the full width at half maxima of the main diffraction peak (β = 0.0436), θ is the main diffraction angle (θ = 13.3°).

Two peaks at 1353 cm−1 and 1570 cm−1 for the Raman spectrum of RCQDs were attributed to the D and G bands respectively (Fig. 2f). The G band (sp2) showed the amount of graphitization, and the D band (sp3) indicated the amount of defects on the surfaces of RCQDs. The intensity ratio of the D band and G band was calculated to be 1.04. The full-survey XPS spectrum of RCQDs showed three elements, C 1s (284.8 eV), N 1s (399.5 eV) and O 1s (532.1 eV) (Fig. 2g). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s was deconvoluted into two peaks, and they were ascribed to –C–C/C=C carbon (284.8 eV) and C–N/C–O carbon (285.4 eV) respectively (Fig. 2h) [31]. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.5 eV and 400.5 eV, they were ascribed to the N atoms from the –NH– group and the graphitic nitrogen (Fig. 2i). The high-resolution spectrum of O 1s was divided into two peaks at 532.1 eV and 533.5 eV, and they were attributed to C–OH and C–O–C respectively (Fig. 2j). The intensity for O 1s was several times of that for N1s, it indicated that there were abundant –OH groups on the surfaces of RCQDs.

The FT-IR spectra for RCQDs isopropanol solution (RCQD-IPA), RCQDs DMF solution (RCQD-DMF), RCQDs acetone solution (RCQD-acetone), RCQDs DMSO solution (RCQD-DMSO) and RCQDs aqueous solution (RCQD-H2O) were obtained by dropping those solutions on a KBr plate respectively, and the KBr plates were dried in air naturally without drying with infrared light. Since KBr was soluble in water, the RCQD-H2O solution was dropped on the KBr plate for several times with each volume of 1 μl after the plate was dried naturally in air. On the KBr plates, some solvent molecules combined with the functional groups (–OH, –NH–) on the surfaces of RCQDs through hydrogen bonds. Both the FT-IR spectrum for RCQDs, and the in-situ FT-IR spectra for RCQD-IPA, RCQD-DMF, RCQD-acetone, RCQD-DMSO and RCQD-H2O are given in Fig. 3 . The FT-IR spectra for RCQDs showed weak characteristic absorption peaks of methylene group at 2924 cm−1 (νas, C–H, –CH2–), 2850 cm−1 (νs, C–H, –CH2–) and 1493 cm−1 (δscissor bending, C–H, –CH2–) together with weak absorption peaks at 753 cm−1 (δ, C–H, –CH2–). Since C–N is polar bond, the absorption peaks for C–N bonds are generally stronger. The strong absorption peaks at 1400 cm−1 and 1325 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration absorptions of aromatic C–N bonds, and it showed that there were two kinds of C–N bonds in larger conjugated structure and smaller conjugated structure. A weak narrow peak at 3344 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration absorption of –NH– (νN-H). The peaks at 1630 cm−1 and 1541 cm−1 were ascribed to the absorptions of the bending vibration from the C–H bonds on aromatic rings. The wide absorption peak centered 3128 cm−1 was attributed to the stretch vibration absorption from –O–H groups (νO-H). It was deduced that there were –NH–, –CH2–, aromatic ring and –OH groups on the surfaces of RCQDs.

Comparing with that for RCQDs, the νO-H peak for RCQD-IPA was broadened greatly due to the H-bonds between isopropanol molecules and the –OH groups. The narrow weak peak of νN-H for RCQD-IPA shifted to 3680 cm−1 from 3344 cm−1. Comparing with that for RCQD-IPA, the νO-H peak for RCQD-DMF was shortened and reduced greatly due to the deprotonation of the –OH groups by DMF molecules, in result the maximum emission wavelength shifted from 608 nm to 504 nm. However, the narrow weak peak of νN-H for RCQD-DMF shifted to 3333 cm−1 from 3344 cm−1. Comparing with that for RCQDs, the νO-H peaks for RCQD-acetone and RCQD-DMSO were broadened also due to the H-bonds between –OH groups and the acetone or DMSO molecules, and the narrow weak peaks of νN-H for RCQD-acetone and RCQD-DMSO shifted to 3742 cm−1 and 3923 cm−1 respectively from 3344 cm−1. However, the νO-H peak for RCQD-H2O was broadened very greatly also due to the strong H-bonds between –OH groups and the H2O molecules. The narrow weak peak of νN-H for RCQD-H2O was not observed, and its wavenumber was supposed to be over 4000 cm−1. Peaks at 952 cm−1, 816 cm−1 and 653 cm−1 were observed for all the samples, but their relative absorption intensities were greatly different.

Surprisingly, the stretch vibration (νO-H) absorption peaks for RCQD-acetone, RCQD-IPA, RCQD-DMSO and RCQD-H2O were observed at 3360 cm−1, 3368 cm−1, 3407 cm−1, 3470 cm−1 respectively, and their maximum emission wavelengths were 592 nm, 608 nm, 610 nm and 624 nm respectively. As for RCQD-acetone, RCQD-IPA, RCQD-DMSO and RCQD-H2O, the solvatochromic phenomenon was deduced that the surface defect states of RCQDs were visibly affected by solvent molecules through the formation of H-bonds between –OH groups on the surfaces of RCQDs and the solvent molecules. As for RCQD-DMF, the reason for the great solvatochromic phenomenon (from 608 nm to 504 nm) was proposed that the –OH groups on the surfaces of RCQDs were deprotonated by DMF molecules.

The UV–vis absorption spectra for RCQDs in water, DMSO, isopropanol, acetone and DMF solutions are given in Fig. 4 . The strong absorption peaks centered at 281 nm was ascribed to the absorption peak of π → π* transitions, and their maximum absorption wavelengths were not affected by solvent molecules. Compared with those for π → π* transitions, the broader and weaker absorption peaks centered at 336 nm was attributed to the absorption peak of n → π* transitions, their maximum absorption wavelengths were not affected by solvent molecules. Since the surface defect states of CQDs are easily affected by the solvent molecules, generally the absorption peaks from the surface defect states are broader and weaker [6,33]. The peaks centered at 608 nm, 570 nm, 569 nm, 550 nm and 478 nm were ascribed to the absorption of the surface defect states for RCQD-H2O, RCQD-DMSO, RCQD-IPA, RCQD-acetone and RCQD-DMF respectively.

However, two peaks at 452 nm and 478 nm were observed for RCQD-DMF, two defect states were deduced to be formed by the deprotonation of the –OH groups on the surfaces of RCQDs by DMF molecules, this phenomenon was consistent with its IR data (Fig. 3). The surface defect state energies for RCQDs in H2O, DMSO, isopropanol, acetone and DMF dilute solutions were determined by the following Eq. (3-2) [34].
(3-2)
ε h ν
2
= B
( h v -
E g
)

In the equation, the ε is molar absorption coefficient, the h is the Planck constant, and the v is the photon’s frequency. The B is a constant, the E g is the surface defect state energy. The UV–vis absorption spectra for RCQDs in different solvent solutions were converted in Fig. 5 based on the equation (3–2). The surface defect state energies for RCQDs in H2O, DMSO, isopropanol, acetone and DMF dilute solutions were determined to be 1.98 eV, 2.07 eV, 2.08 eV, 2.14 eV and 2.47 eV respectively. However, the surface defect state energy for RCQDs was greatly affected by the solvent molecules. The E g values were close to the values which were calculated by 1241/the maximum emission wavelengths (nm) (Table S1).

As shown in Fig. 6 a, the maximum excitation wavelengths for RCQDs in DMF, acetone, isopropanol, DMSO and water dilute solutions were 479 nm, 553 nm, 571 nm, 572 nm and 576 nm respectively. However, two visible maximum wavelengths for RCQDs DMF solution were observed, and this phenomenon was consistent with the absorption peaks from its surface defect states (Fig. 4). The maximum emission wavelengths for RCQDs in DMF, acetone, isopropanol, DMSO and water dilute solutions were 504 nm, 592 nm, 608 nm, 610 nm and 624 nm respectively (Fig. 6b). Compared with that for RCQDs isopropanol solution, the maximum emission intensities for RCQDs in DMF, acetone, DMSO and water solutions were 133 %, 96.3 %, 78.5 % and 10.4 % respectively.

The optimum synthesis temperature (130 °C) for RCQDs was obtained with the same synthesis time of 1 h, and the optimum synthesis time (1 h) was got with the same synthesis temperature of 130 °C (Fig. S1). The pyrolysis of the precursors of N1-phenylbenzene-1,2,4-triamine and isopropanol molecules (also as solvent) were catalyzed greatly by the SnO2 NPs which were synchronously synthesized, and both the synthesis time and the synthesis temperature were greatly reduced. The red emission for RCQDs isopropanol solution with higher luminescent efficiency was attributed to that larger rigid π-conjugated skeleton structure might be formed with N1-phenylbenzene-1,2,4-triamine as one of the precursors [15]. All the emission spectra for RCQDs in DMF, acetone, isopropanol, DMSO and water dilute solutions were independent on the excitation wavelengths (Fig. S2) showing good optical homogeneity which originated from the surface defect states [35,36]. The emission intensity for RCQDs isopropanol solution was not observed to decline in 30 days in air, and it showed good stability (Fig. S3).

Interestingly, the maximum emission wavelengths shifted 120 nm from 624 nm for RCQDs aqueous solution to 504 nm for RCQDs DMF solution, RCQDs showed surprising solvatochromic properties in solvents (Fig. S4) [37]. The CIE coordinates of the emission spectra for RCQDs in DMF, acetone, isopropanol, DMSO and water dilute solutions were (0.203, 0.635), (0.588, 0.410), (0.643, 0.355), (0.644, 0.353) and (0.664, 0.337) respectively (Fig. S5). Surprisingly, compared with those for RCQDs isopropanol solution, the maximum emission wavelength for RCQDs DMF solution blue shifted 104 nm, and the maximum emission intensity for RCQDs DMF solution was increased 33 %. These great changes were ascribed to the deprotonation of the –OH groups on the surfaces on RCQDs by DMF molecules, and the surface defect state energy for RCQDs DMF solution was increased greatly. Moreover, the surface defect states for RCQDs DMF dilute solution was stabilized greatly.

Both dipole–dipole and hydrogen bonding (HB) interactions lead to red-shift of luminescence emission [38]. The emission and normalized emission spectra for 0.040 mg/ml RCQDs in toluene, DCM, IPA, THF, EtOH, EA, acetone, MeOH, ACN, DMF, DMSO and water are given in Fig. S6, and the photos for RCQDs in eleven solvents under daylight and 365 nm light are given in Fig. S7. RCQDs could not be dispersed in hexane, and only some of RCQDs could be dispersed in toluene and DCM with lower luminescence intensities due to their poor dispersibility (Fig. S7). Aggregate was not observed for RCQDs in ethanol and isopropanol dilute solutions for more than three months. The luminescence properties for RCQDs in thirteen solvents are given in Table S2. The maximum emission wavelengths for RCQDs in methanol, ethanol and isopropanol dilute solutions were centered around at 608 nm, compared with that for RCQDs isopropanol solution, the maximum emission intensities for RCQDs in methanol and ethanol were 94.8 % and 96.3 % respectively (Table S2). As for the above mentioned alcohols, the polarity of the solvents just affected the emission intensities slightly. Moreover, their maximum emission wavelengths were centered around at 608 nm. Based on the experiments’ results, the patterns or the vibration modes of the hydrogen bonds between the functional groups of RCQDs and the solvent molecules were inferred to affect the maximum emission wavelengths and the emission intensities visibly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quantum yields and the lifetimes
ce:section.ce:para: The absolute quantum yields for RCQDs in DMF, acetone, isopropanol, DMSO, and H2O were determined to be 55.2 %, 36.9 %, 43.9 %, 32.1 % and 8.1 %, respectively, and the quantum data were given in Table 1 . The full width at half maxima (FWHM) data for RCQDs in DMF, acetone, isopropanol, DMSO and water dilute solutions were given in Table 1. However, as the FWHM value increased, the absolute quantum yield decreased. The quantum yield for RCQDs DMF solution was the highest (55.2 %) with the lowest FWHM value of 41 nm. As the hydrogen bonds between the functional groups (–OH) on the surfaces of RCQDs and the solvent molecules increased, the characteristic infrared absorption peaks broadened wider (see Fig. 3) together with lower quantum yields and larger wavenumbers for the stretch vibration of the functional groups of –NH– on the surfaces of RCQDs.

Surprisingly, as the characteristic infrared absorption bands of –OH groups on the surfaces of RCQDs narrowed, the FWHM values decreased visibly. The broad infrared absorption band of the –OH group showed stronger vibration between the RCQDs and the solvent molecules through the hydrogen bonds. Moreover, as the hydrogen bond vibration between the RCQDs and the solvent molecules increased, the quantum yields for RCQDs in dilute solutions decreased simultaneously.

The lifetime decay curves for RCQDs in different solvent solutions were recorded by monitoring the maximum emission intensities with EPL 450 nm laser as the excitation light source, the lifetime decay curves were given in Fig. 7 .

The lifetime data was fitted automatically by FLS 1000 instrument. The decay curves for RCQDs in DMF and isopropanol solutions were fitted perfectly in the first exponential decay equation, and the equation was given in Eq. (3-3).
(3-3)
I F
=
I
F 0
+
A 1
e
- t / τ 1

The decay curves for RCQDs in acetone, DMSO and water dilute solutions were fitted perfectly in the second exponential decay equation, and the equation was given in Eq. (3-4).
(3-4)
I F
=
I
F 0
+
A 1
e
( - t / τ 1 )
+
A 2
e
( - t / τ 2 )

Here, the I F is the emission intensity, the t is the decay time. The I F0 is the initial luminescence intensity, the A 1 and A 2 are the percentages for the first decay lifetime and the second decay lifetime respectively. The τ 1 and τ 2 are the first decay lifetime and the second decay lifetime respectively. All the lifetime data were obtained by FLS 1000 instrument automatically, and the lifetime data were given in Table 2 .

The lifetime decay curves for RCQDs in DMF and isopropanol dilute solutions were fitted in the first exponential decay equation with higher quantum yields. However, the lifetime decay curves for RCQDs in acetone, DMSO and water dilute solutions were fitted in the second exponential decay equation with relatively lower quantum yields. Particularly for RCQDs aqueous solution the A 2 value was increased greatly to 74.0 % with the lowest quantum yield of 8.1 %. The stronger vibration patterns of the hydrogen bonds between the functional groups of RCQDs and the solvent molecules might inspire a new non-radiative electron transfer mode, which might lower the quantum yield of RCQDs greatly.

As shown in Fig. 8 a and b, the maximum emission intensities of RCQDs remained nearly the same values in the pH range of 4.8 ∼ 5.5. Furthermore, the emission intensities for RCQDs isopropanol solution were affected by the content of PBS buffer solution (pH = 5.0) (Fig. 8c), and the luminescence intensities of RCQDs isopropanol solution remained stable (I/I0 = 0.87) as the content of the PBS buffer solution (pH = 5.0) were 40 V% ∼ 60 V% (Fig. 8d). Here the I 0 was the luminescence intensity for pure RCQDs isopropanol solution, and the I was the luminescence intensity for RCQDs isopropanol solution mixed with different content of PBS buffer solution or mixed with MnO4 −, other anions and cations. The luminescence of RCQDs isopropanol solution was quenched quickly by 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l MnO4 − (Fig. 8e), and the luminescence intensities remained stable after 3 min (Fig. 8f).

The luminescence intensities for RCQDs in the mixed PBS buffer solution decreased with the increasing concentration of MnO4 − (Fig. 9 a), and the curve for the I/I0 versus the concentration of MnO4 − was fitted perfectly in the equation of (3-5) (Fig. 9b).
(3-5)
I /
I 0
= k + A
e
- c / c 0
Here, the k was the terminal value of the I/I 0 after 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs was mixed with high concentration of MnO4 − in PBS buffer solution. The A was a constant, and the I/I 0 value declined quickly with larger value of A. The c was the concentration of MnO4 −, and the c 0 was the concentration of MnO4 − as the value of I/I 0 declined to be 1/e. In Eq. (3-5), k = 0.07 ± 0.01, A = 0.94 ± 0.01, c 0 = 4.48 ± 0.17 μM.

As the concentration of MnO4 − was in the range of 1.0 × 10−7 mol/l ∼ 2.0 × 10−6 mol/l, the I/I 0 value declined linearly with the concentration of MnO4 − (Fig. 9c), and it was fitted perfectly in the equation of (3-6).
(3-6)
I /
I 0
= a + b c

Here, a and b were constants, and c was the concentration of MnO4 −, a = 1.006 ± 0.005, b = -0.181 ± 0.007, R 2 = 0.991. The limit of the detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.1 μM using the standard deviation method (LOD = 3σ/k).

Fig. 10 gives the selectivity experiment results for some cations and anions mixed with 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs in PBS buffer solutions. As shown in Fig. 10a, the luminescence intensity for 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs was largely quenched by 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l MnO4 −. The I/I0 values decreased slightly after the RCQDs solutions were mixed with 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l Cu2+ (0.909) or 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l Fe3+ (0.919). The I/I0 values did not decline visibly after the RCQDs solutions were mixed with 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l K+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Ba2+ and Na+ ions respectively. As for the anions, the I/I0 values decreased slightly after the RCQDs solutions were mixed with 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l Cl− (0.983) or 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l Cr2O7 2− (0.926), no significant decrease of the I/I0 values was found after the RCQDs solutions were mixed with 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l F−, Br−, CO3 2−, HCO3 2−, HPO4 2−, NO3 −, NO2 −, SO4 2−, C2O4 2− and S2O8 2− respectively (Fig. 10b). In addition, interference tests under coexistence of MnO4 − and some cations or under coexistence of MnO4 − and some anions (Fig. 10c and 10d). Only Cu2+, Cr2O7 2− ions slightly interfere the detection of MnO4 −.

Generally, the quenching mechanisms for luminescent carbon quantum dots are photoinduced electron transfer (PET), internal filtration effect (IFE), resonance energy transfer (RET), dynamic quenching and static quenching [39–41]. The absorption band of MnO4 − in range of 500 nm ∼ 600 nm overlapped the absorption band of 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs solution largely. As 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs solution was mixed with 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l MnO4 − in PBS buffer solution, the maximum absorption peak at 572 nm shifted to 546 nm, moreover, its absorbance at 546 nm decreased 37 % compared with that at 572 nm for 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs solution (Fig. 11 a).

However, the lifetime decay curve for 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs solution mixed with 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l MnO4 − was close to that for 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs solution, and their average lifetimes were 2.68 ns and 2.74 ns respectively (Fig. 11b). The lifetime decay curve for 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs solution mixed with 1.0 × 10−5 mol/l MnO4 − was fitted perfectly in the second exponential decay equation (Eq. (3)-(4), but the lifetime decay curve for 0.020 mg/ml RCQDs solution was fitted perfectly in the first exponential decay equation (equation 3–3). The lifetime data were given in Table S3. As for MnO4 − ions, the quenching mechanism for RCQDs solution was proposed mainly to be internal filtering effect (IFE) [42]. Some of the excited electrons on the excited state of RCQDs might transfer to the excited state of MnO4 − ions through RET process, and the luminescence of RCQDs was quenched by the latter through non-radiative process. Moreover, the lifetime decay curve for the mixed solution was the second exponential decay process due to the RET process together slightly lowed lifetime. In result, RCQDs solution in PBS buffer solution showed good selectivity for detecting MnO4 − ions.

Red luminescent carbon quantum dots isopropanol solution was synthesized using isopropanol as carbon source and solvent, N1-phenylbenzene-1,2,4-triamine as carbon and nitrogen source. The synthesis was perfectly catalyzed by SnO2 NPs, and the solvothermal synthesis time and temperature were greatly reduced. The RCQDs showed wider solvatochromic properties (120 nm). As for RCQDs in acetone, isopropanol, DMSO and water solutions, the formation of strong hydrogen bonds between the functional –OH groups on the surfaces of RCQDs and the solvent molecules inspired the solvatochromic properties. As for RCQDs DMF solution, the deprotonation of the –OH functional groups on the surfaces of RCQDs inspired its large blue shift of the maximum emission wavelength (from 624 nm to 504 nm). Compared with those for RCQDs isopropanol solution, the quantum yield for RCQDs DMF solution was increased 1.33 times together with the FWHM decreased 0.87 times through the deprotonation reaction. As the vibration of the hydrogen bonds between the functional groups of RCQDs and the solvent molecules increased, the quantum yield of RCQDs in dilute solutions decreased visibly. As the infrared absorption bands of the –OH groups on the surfaces of RCQDs narrowed, the FWHM values decreased visibly together with increased quantum yields. MnO4 − ions could be selectively detected using RCQDs in PBS buffer solution (pH = 5.0), and the linear range was 1.0 × 10−7 mol/l ∼ 2.0 × 10−6 mol/l.

Wen-Xin Chu: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Xiao-Min Tang: Data curation. Fei-Yang Lu: Data curation. Bao-Li An: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Ji-Ming Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Xiao-Hong Wang: Formal analysis. Yue-Ling Bai: Formal analysis. Jiaqiang Xu: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.62271299). The authors also acknowledge the Instrumental Analysis and Research Center of Shanghai University for providing measurement services.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2025.116288.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient one step synthesis of green carbon quantum dots catalyzed by tin oxide
